text: Management of refractory GERD after sleeve gastrectomy conversion to bypass. The patient is a forty-five-year-old female with history of hypertension and hypothyroidism. She underwent a laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy two years ago. One year after the surgery, patient complained of GERD symptoms and food regurgitation. She was managed on four oral PPIs for six months with no relief of symptoms. BMI decreased from forty-six to thirty-one, but symptoms of heartburn were persistent. EGD showed mild gastritis in the antrum with no ulceration. Manometry study showed low pressures at the GE junction with normal peristalsis. DeMeester score was twenty-nine.
The surgical team places trocars in the patient's abdomen for robotic revisional surgery, preparing for the conversion from sleeve gastrectomy to gastric bypass.

The surgeon performs a diagnostic laparoscopy, revealing multiple adhesions from previous surgery, which are lysed using a monopolar hook. A posterior window is opened, and a penrose drain is placed for traction and exposure. Dissection progresses into the mediastinum, fully mobilizing the esophagus. The diaphragmatic pillars are dissected on both anterior and posterior aspects, and the hiatus is repaired with four interrupted stitches of zero non-absorbable suture. The gastric tube is completely dissected, and two stapler loads are used to transect it.

The surgeon retracts the transverse colon to identify the angle of Treitz. The jejunum is measured and transected with a stapler 50 centimeters from the angle. A 120-centimeter segment of the alimentary limb is prepared. Enterotomies are created using a monopolar hook, followed by a side-to-side anastomosis with a stapler. The enterotomies are then over-sewn in two layers using a 3-0 absorbable suture.

The surgeon begins the gastrojejunostomy by suturing a continuous posterior outer layer using 3-0 absorbable suture. Enterotomies are created with a monopolar hook. A continuous inner-posterior layer is then sutured, followed by an inner-anterior layer. To enhance reconstruction, a suture is started from the opposite side and joined with the previous one at the midpoint. The procedure concludes with the completion of the outer anterior layer, finalizing the anastomosis.

The OR time was a hundred and fifty minutes. The estimated blood loss, ten cc's. No complications were registered and the patient was discharged on post-op day one. After one month follow-up, patient refers improvement of the symptoms.
